name: Vipont Mine
short_description: BLM-managed rockhounding site in the Bear River Mountains at 7,400 ft. Collect quartz and feldspar specimens on public land with no permit required.
description:
Vipont Mine is a publicly accessible rockhounding location on Bureau of Land Management property in Box Elder County's high country. Situated at 7,419 feet elevation on gentle slopes, the site offers collectors the opportunity to find quartz, feldspar, and mica specimens typical of the Precambrian metamorphic rocks exposed in Utah's northern mountains.

The Bear River Mountains in this region contain ancient metamorphic basement rocks that have been uplifted and exposed by millions of years of erosion. The mineral assemblages here reflect the high-grade metamorphic conditions that formed these rocks during the Precambrian era, when intense heat and pressure transformed sedimentary and igneous protoliths into crystalline metamorphic gneiss and schist. Quartz veins are common throughout the area, often accompanied by pink feldspar and silvery mica minerals.

This location attracts rockhounds seeking quality quartz crystals and feldspar specimens in a relatively undisturbed setting. The gentle terrain and moderate elevation make it accessible to most collectors, though the high altitude means afternoon thunderstorms are possible in summer months. The site's remote location means you should come prepared with adequate water and supplies.

Collectors typically find the best specimens by working along erosional washes and examining rocky outcrops where weathering has exposed fresh mineral surfaces. The combination of abundant quartz veins and the site's manageable terrain makes it a solid choice for intermediate rockhounds seeking quality alpine specimens.
history: The Vipont Mine area sits within the Bear River Mountains, which expose Precambrian metamorphic rocks formed during major crustal deformation approximately 1.6 to 2.7 billion years ago. The granite and metamorphic gneiss exposed at this elevation record ancient episodes of continental collision and metamorphism that created the quartz-rich mineral assemblages found here today.
tips: Search along rocky washes and erosional gullies where weathering has exposed fresh quartz veins; feldspar crystals often occur alongside quartz in pink and white colorations. Bring a 10x hand lens to examine crystal faces and identify feldspar vs. quartz in the field - feldspar will show striations and a duller luster than quartz. Work the north and east-facing slopes in late morning when sunlight shows mineral glint; bring plenty of water as the 7,400 ft elevation means dehydration risk and afternoon thunderstorm potential in summer.
terrain_notes: Elevation 7,419 feet on gentle slopes typical of high mountain terrain. Rocky, uneven footing with exposed metamorphic outcrops and scattered boulder fields. Sparse subalpine vegetation. No developed trails; expect 0.5 to 2 miles of hiking from parking depending on target area. Walking surfaces are rocky and require sturdy footwear.
best_season: June - September. High elevation means deep snow blocks access October through May. Summer months offer dry conditions and stable weather, though afternoon thunderstorms are common in July-August. September offers clearest skies and lower storm risk.
written_directions: From Logan, Utah, take US 89 north toward Cache Valley. Continue to Brigham City and head east into Box Elder County via local roads toward the Wellsville Mountains and Bear River Mountain access. The Vipont Mine site is accessed via rough backcountry roads north of Wellsville. Coordinates 41.965705, -113.845922 point to the mining area. High-clearance vehicle recommended. Consult current BLM maps as road conditions change seasonally. No reliable trailhead signs present; GPS navigation essential.
road_conditions: Rough backcountry roads with potential washouts and seasonal damage. High-clearance all-wheel-drive vehicle recommended. Road may be impassable during spring snowmelt and winter months.
parking_notes: Rough parking area or pullout near mine site. Limited capacity. No developed facilities.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 lbs per day per Federal Land Policy and Management Act (FLPMA). No commercial collection. No motorized excavation. No removal of rock from active mining claims if any are present. Respect all posted claim markers.
rules:
Personal use collecting only
25 lb daily limit per FLPMA
No commercial collection
No motorized excavation equipment
No disturbance of mining claims
Pack out all waste
nearest_services: Brigham City, Utah approximately 25 miles south. Provides gas, food, and supplies. Logan, Utah approximately 30 miles south offers full services.
accessibility_notes: Uneven rocky terrain with loose stones and scattered boulders. Rough backcountry access road requires high-clearance vehicle. No wheelchair access. High elevation (7,400 ft) demands physical fitness and acclimatization. Sturdy hiking boots and trekking poles recommended. Not suitable for limited mobility or those with altitude sensitivity.

faq.question: What gems and minerals can you find at Vipont Mine in Utah?
faq.answer: Vipont Mine is known for producing quartz crystals, pink and white feldspar specimens, and silvery mica flakes. The site's exposure of Precambrian metamorphic rocks makes it an excellent location for collectors seeking high-quality quartz specimens and feldspar crystals typical of Utah's northern mountains.

faq.question: Do you need a permit to rockhound at Vipont Mine?
faq.answer: No permit is required. Vipont Mine sits on Bureau of Land Management (BLM) public land in Box Elder County where personal-use rockhounding is allowed under Federal Land Policy and Management Act guidelines. Simply follow the 25 lb daily collection limit and no-commercial-use rules.

faq.question: How much quartz and feldspar can you collect per day at Vipont Mine?
faq.answer: You can collect a maximum of 25 pounds per day at Vipont Mine under BLM personal-use collecting rules. This limit applies to all hand-collected specimens combined, so plan your trip accordingly and bring a reliable scale or estimate weights carefully.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to rockhound for quartz at Vipont Mine?
faq.answer: June through September is the optimal season. The site sits at 7,419 feet elevation where heavy snow blocks access October through May. Summer months offer dry conditions and stable weather, though afternoon thunderstorms are common in July-August. September provides the clearest skies and lowest storm risk.

faq.question: Is Vipont Mine suitable for beginner rockhounders or families with children?
faq.answer: Vipont Mine is best suited for intermediate to advanced collectors aged 12 and older. The remote backcountry location, high elevation (7,419 ft), rough terrain, and demanding road access make it more challenging than beginner-friendly fee dig sites. Families should have hiking experience and proper equipment before visiting.

faq.question: What vehicle and tools do you need to rockhound near Brigham City at Vipont Mine?
faq.answer: A high-clearance all-wheel-drive vehicle is essential for accessing the rough backcountry roads. Bring a geologist's hammer, chisel, 10x hand lens for identifying feldspar, sturdy hiking boots, trekking poles, abundant water (dehydration risk at high elevation), a backpack for specimens, and GPS navigation as trail markers are absent.

meta_description: Find quartz, feldspar, and mica at Vipont Mine near Brigham City, Utah. BLM public land, no permit required. 25 lb daily limit. High elevation alpine site.
needs_review_fields: written_directions (backcountry location requires verification of current road access)
